CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and reads clearly — intro, method (LSTM-Jump with REINFORCE), experiments and conclusion are presented logically, and writing quality is reported as good. The main contributions (learning discrete jumps, REINFORCE training, 6x speedups across tasks) are stated early and repeatedly, and related work coverage appears thorough. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate for reproducibility, and figures/tables/examples (datasets and tasks) are informative, though the summary does not indicate whether code or full implementation details are provided. Overall presentation is strong with only minor omissions around implementation-release clarity that would aid reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states the main contributions early — proposing LSTM-Jump and training discrete jumps with REINFORCE — making the approach easy to follow. Notation and experimental details (datasets, hyperparameters, baselines) are reported and related work coverage is thorough, which aids reproducibility. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is generally good, though reliance on policy gradient and brief notes on overhead suggest a few places where more clarity or expanded discussion would further improve readability.

Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate for reproducibility, and figures/tables/examples (datasets and tasks) are informative, though the summary does not indicate whether code or full implementation details are provided. Overall presentation is strong with only minor omissions around implementation-release clarity that would aid reproducibility.

Notation and experimental details (datasets, hyperparameters, baselines) are reported and related work coverage is thorough, which aids reproducibility. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This work addresses an important, venue-relevant problem (efficient text processing via learned skipping) and evaluates on standard NLP benchmarks (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) with accuracy and runtime speedup metrics, which suits a top NLP/ML conference audience. The methodology (LSTM-Jump with REINFORCE) and comparison to vanilla LSTMs are appropriate, but practical concerns—policy-gradient training variability, noted overhead for small models, and no mention of released code—leave some reproducibility and engineering questions that prevent a perfect fit. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it proposes a new recurrent architecture (LSTM-Jump) that learns discrete skipping via policy gradient and evaluates on standard NLP benchmarks (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) with both accuracy and runtime speedup metrics. The problem formulation, methodology (REINFORCE for non-differentiable jumps), and baselines (vanilla LSTM) match community expectations, and the tasks (classification and reading comprehension) target the core audience. Positioning and related work coverage are reported as thorough, making the contribution relevant and of clear interest to the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses an important, venue-relevant problem (efficient text processing via learned skipping) and evaluates on standard NLP benchmarks (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) with accuracy and runtime speedup metrics, which suits a top NLP/ML conference audience. The methodology (LSTM-Jump with REINFORCE) and comparison to vanilla LSTMs are appropriate, but practical concerns—policy-gradient training variability, noted overhead for small models, and no mention of released code—leave some reproducibility and engineering questions that prevent a perfect fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it proposes a new recurrent architecture (LSTM-Jump) that learns discrete skipping via policy gradient and evaluates on standard NLP benchmarks (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) with both accuracy and runtime speedup metrics. The problem formulation, methodology (REINFORCE for non-differentiable jumps), and baselines (vanilla LSTM) match community expectations, and the tasks (classification and reading comprehension) target the core audience. Positioning and related work coverage are reported as thorough, making the contribution relevant and of clear interest to the venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper proposes a concrete modification (LSTM-Jump) that learns discrete token-skipping decisions via REINFORCE, which is a clear and practical extension of sequential LSTMs and hard-attention ideas. However, the core concepts—selective reading/skipping and policy-gradient for discrete control—are existing techniques applied to RNN reading, so the contribution is a moderate, non-trivial engineering/algorithmic extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do differentiate their method empirically (multiple datasets and reported speedups up to 6x) which would be informative to practitioners, but it does not appear to introduce radically new theory or problem formulation. | B(4): The paper proposes a concrete mechanism (LSTM-Jump) that augments LSTMs with learned discrete jumps trained via REINFORCE to skip tokens, which is a notable and practical extension beyond vanilla sequential LSTMs. While skipping/conditional computation and hard-attention ideas have precedents, the specific combination—per-token jump softmax over 1..K, sampling jumps, and empirical demonstration of multi-task speedups (up to 6x) on classification and QA datasets—is a clear, non-trivial technical contribution that is well-differentiated from the baseline comparisons. An NLP expert would find this informative and sufficiently novel in methodology and empirical focus, though it sits within an existing family of adaptive computation/hard-attention approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete modification (LSTM-Jump) that learns discrete token-skipping decisions via REINFORCE, which is a clear and practical extension of sequential LSTMs and hard-attention ideas. However, the core concepts—selective reading/skipping and policy-gradient for discrete control—are existing techniques applied to RNN reading, so the contribution is a moderate, non-trivial engineering/algorithmic extension rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. The authors do differentiate their method empirically (multiple datasets and reported speedups up to 6x) which would be informative to practitioners, but it does not appear to introduce radically new theory or problem formulation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a concrete mechanism (LSTM-Jump) that augments LSTMs with learned discrete jumps trained via REINFORCE to skip tokens, which is a notable and practical extension beyond vanilla sequential LSTMs. While skipping/conditional computation and hard-attention ideas have precedents, the specific combination—per-token jump softmax over 1..K, sampling jumps, and empirical demonstration of multi-task speedups (up to 6x) on classification and QA datasets—is a clear, non-trivial technical contribution that is well-differentiated from the baseline comparisons. An NLP expert would find this informative and sufficiently novel in methodology and empirical focus, though it sits within an existing family of adaptive computation/hard-attention approaches.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates LSTM-Jump against a vanilla sequential LSTM on multiple datasets (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) and reports accuracy and runtime speedups, which is useful, but it omits comparisons to other recent speedup or conditional computation approaches (e.g., attention‑based short‑circuiting, hierarchical/skip RNNs or adaptive computation time variants). Comparisons appear fair in using the same datasets and metrics and the authors honestly note limitations of policy‑gradient training, but the lack of stronger competing baselines means the empirical evaluation is incomplete for judging practical improvements over the state of the art. | B(3): The paper evaluates on several datasets and reports accuracy and runtime speedups against a vanilla sequential LSTM using the same data and metrics, so comparisons are fair in that limited scope. However, it only uses the standard LSTM as a baseline, omitting other relevant recent approaches (e.g., soft/hard attention models, hierarchical/skip RNNs, adaptive computation or conditional computation methods) that directly address skipping or adaptive reading. The manuscript does discuss related work thoroughly and notes training drawbacks of policy gradient, but empirical comparisons to those competing methods are missing, leaving an important gap in assessing practical gains and trade-offs.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates LSTM-Jump against a vanilla sequential LSTM on multiple datasets (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) and reports accuracy and runtime speedups, which is useful, but it omits comparisons to other recent speedup or conditional computation approaches (e.g., attention‑based short‑circuiting, hierarchical/skip RNNs or adaptive computation time variants). Comparisons appear fair in using the same datasets and metrics and the authors honestly note limitations of policy‑gradient training, but the lack of stronger competing baselines means the empirical evaluation is incomplete for judging practical improvements over the state of the art.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on several datasets and reports accuracy and runtime speedups against a vanilla sequential LSTM using the same data and metrics, so comparisons are fair in that limited scope. However, it only uses the standard LSTM as a baseline, omitting other relevant recent approaches (e.g., soft/hard attention models, hierarchical/skip RNNs, adaptive computation or conditional computation methods) that directly address skipping or adaptive reading. The manuscript does discuss related work thoroughly and notes training drawbacks of policy gradient, but empirical comparisons to those competing methods are missing, leaving an important gap in assessing practical gains and trade-offs.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper clearly states its main claims (LSTM-Jump that skips tokens, trained with REINFORCE, achieving up to 6x runtime speedup while maintaining accuracy) and provides experimental evidence across several datasets (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) comparing to vanilla LSTM. However, from the summary I note gaps in experimental rigor: no mention of released code, limited baseline diversity beyond vanilla LSTM, and no reported statistical significance or sensitivity analyses for the policy-gradient training (variance, seeds, or ablations on K and jump policy). The mathematical treatment appears appropriate for applying REINFORCE to discrete jumps, but the reliance on policy gradients and potential training instability is acknowledged and not fully diagnosed, so the conclusions are supported but would benefit from additional reproducibility and robustness checks. | B(4): The paper's main claims (LSTM-Jump using REINFORCE to learn discrete jumps, achieving up to 6x speedups while retaining accuracy on several datasets including AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, and CBT) are supported by experiments and reported hyperparameters. Mathematical components are straightforward (policy gradient for non-differentiable jumps) and no obvious derivation errors are indicated. However, the experimental methodology is somewhat narrow—comparisons appear limited to a vanilla LSTM baseline, there is no mention of statistical significance or variance reporting, and REINFORCE-related training variance and overhead are acknowledged but not fully analyzed. Overall the technical evidence largely supports the claims but some empirical gaps and potential instability of the training procedure temper confidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (LSTM-Jump that skips tokens, trained with REINFORCE, achieving up to 6x runtime speedup while maintaining accuracy) and provides experimental evidence across several datasets (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT) comparing to vanilla LSTM. However, from the summary I note gaps in experimental rigor: no mention of released code, limited baseline diversity beyond vanilla LSTM, and no reported statistical significance or sensitivity analyses for the policy-gradient training (variance, seeds, or ablations on K and jump policy). The mathematical treatment appears appropriate for applying REINFORCE to discrete jumps, but the reliance on policy gradients and potential training instability is acknowledged and not fully diagnosed, so the conclusions are supported but would benefit from additional reproducibility and robustness checks.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (LSTM-Jump using REINFORCE to learn discrete jumps, achieving up to 6x speedups while retaining accuracy on several datasets including AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, and CBT) are supported by experiments and reported hyperparameters. Mathematical components are straightforward (policy gradient for non-differentiable jumps) and no obvious derivation errors are indicated. However, the experimental methodology is somewhat narrow—comparisons appear limited to a vanilla LSTM baseline, there is no mention of statistical significance or variance reporting, and REINFORCE-related training variance and overhead are acknowledged but not fully analyzed. Overall the technical evidence largely supports the claims but some empirical gaps and potential instability of the training procedure temper confidence.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates LSTM-Jump across a range of tasks (synthetic number prediction, sentiment and news classification, CBT QA) and reports speedups and accuracy, which supports the core claim of faster inference without accuracy loss. However, from the summary there are limited ablation/sensitivity analyses on key design choices (e.g., effect of K, jump policy variance, number of policy-gradient samples, multiple seeds) and engineering details (compute, wall-clock vs. FLOPs, code) appear missing, which hinders reproducibility and assessment of robustness. Related work coverage and explicit limitations (policy gradient downside, overhead for small models) are acknowledged, but more systematic diagnostics and release of code/compute details would be needed for a higher score. | B(4): The experiments cover a diverse set of tasks (synthetic number prediction, sentiment/classification datasets, and CBT QA) and report both accuracy and runtime speedups up to 6x, supporting the main claims. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear limited: only vanilla LSTM is used as a baseline rather than other skipping/conditional computation methods, and overhead effects are discussed but not exhaustively ablated across architectures. Related work is reported as thorough, and limitations (policy gradient training cost and small-model overhead) are acknowledged; hyperparameters are reported but code availability is not mentioned, so full reproducibility may require effort. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid with some important comparative and ablation gaps left unexamined.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates LSTM-Jump across a range of tasks (synthetic number prediction, sentiment and news classification, CBT QA) and reports speedups and accuracy, which supports the core claim of faster inference without accuracy loss. However, from the summary there are limited ablation/sensitivity analyses on key design choices (e.g., effect of K, jump policy variance, number of policy-gradient samples, multiple seeds) and engineering details (compute, wall-clock vs. FLOPs, code) appear missing, which hinders reproducibility and assessment of robustness. Related work coverage and explicit limitations (policy gradient downside, overhead for small models) are acknowledged, but more systematic diagnostics and release of code/compute details would be needed for a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments cover a diverse set of tasks (synthetic number prediction, sentiment/classification datasets, and CBT QA) and report both accuracy and runtime speedups up to 6x, supporting the main claims. Ablations and sensitivity analyses appear limited: only vanilla LSTM is used as a baseline rather than other skipping/conditional computation methods, and overhead effects are discussed but not exhaustively ablated across architectures. Related work is reported as thorough, and limitations (policy gradient training cost and small-model overhead) are acknowledged; hyperparameters are reported but code availability is not mentioned, so full reproducibility may require effort. Overall the empirical evaluation is solid with some important comparative and ablation gaps left unexamined.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper targets an important practical problem — reducing sequential reading cost in RNNs — and demonstrates meaningful runtime gains (up to 6x) while preserving accuracy across several standard datasets (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT). However, the contribution is an incremental architectural/optimization improvement rather than a paradigm shift: it relies on policy-gradient training (a noted limitation) and compares mainly against vanilla LSTMs, which limits immediate broad adoption in the presence of competing efficient architectures. Overall, this is a useful and practically relevant idea that will likely inspire follow-up work but is unlikely by itself to fundamentally change how researchers approach efficient sequence modeling. | B(4): This paper tackles the important problem of efficiency in sequence modeling by learning discrete jumps (LSTM-Jump) and demonstrates substantive runtime gains (up to 6x) while preserving accuracy across diverse tasks (number prediction, sentiment, news classification, CBT QA). Its empirical evidence that learned skipping can be integrated into LSTM workflows and applied to both classification and QA is likely to change how researchers approach adaptive computation for long texts and inspires followups on learned control in reading models. The reliance on REINFORCE and reported overheads for small models temper the impact slightly, but overall this work opens useful research directions and is likely to be built upon in efficiency- and interpretability-focused NLP research.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important practical problem — reducing sequential reading cost in RNNs — and demonstrates meaningful runtime gains (up to 6x) while preserving accuracy across several standard datasets (AG News, IMDB, Rotten Tomatoes, CBT). However, the contribution is an incremental architectural/optimization improvement rather than a paradigm shift: it relies on policy-gradient training (a noted limitation) and compares mainly against vanilla LSTMs, which limits immediate broad adoption in the presence of competing efficient architectures. Overall, this is a useful and practically relevant idea that will likely inspire follow-up work but is unlikely by itself to fundamentally change how researchers approach efficient sequence modeling.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important problem of efficiency in sequence modeling by learning discrete jumps (LSTM-Jump) and demonstrates substantive runtime gains (up to 6x) while preserving accuracy across diverse tasks (number prediction, sentiment, news classification, CBT QA). Its empirical evidence that learned skipping can be integrated into LSTM workflows and applied to both classification and QA is likely to change how researchers approach adaptive computation for long texts and inspires followups on learned control in reading models. The reliance on REINFORCE and reported overheads for small models temper the impact slightly, but overall this work opens useful research directions and is likely to be built upon in efficiency- and interpretability-focused NLP research.

main_claims: Proposes LSTM-Jump, a modified LSTM that learns to skip tokens by jumping over portions of text.
Trains the discrete jumping decision using policy gradient (REINFORCE) to handle non-differentiability.
Achieves up to 6x speedup over standard sequential LSTM while maintaining same or better accuracy on several tasks.
Demonstrates applicability on tasks including number prediction, sentiment analysis, news article classification, and automatic Q&A.
_summary.methodology: An LSTM-based recurrent network reads R tokens, computes a jump softmax over 1..K, samples a jump κ to skip ahead, and repeats until stop conditions; discrete jump decisions are trained with a REINFORCE policy gradient method.
limitations: Method requires policy gradient training, which the authors note is a disadvantage compared to some alternatives.
When embedding size and hidden units are small, overhead from calculating and sampling the jump softmax can dominate and reduce speedup.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - document classification and reading comprehension / question answering

reviewer_a.name: Practical Evaluator
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 8+ years building and benchmarking NLP systems in both research and industry, with a focus on reproducible experimentation and deployment performance.
traits: expects clear, reproducible experimental setups (hyperparameters, seeds, compute) and values open-source code and instructions to reproduce results
looks for strong baselines, ablation studies, evaluation on varied datasets, and sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of K, jump policy randomness, training stability)
checks for correct use of statistical comparisons and consistent reporting of speedups (wall-clock vs. FLOPs vs. tokens/sec) and memory/latency trade-offs
notices engineering details that affect results (initialization, variance of REINFORCE, number of policy gradient samples, curriculum or pretraining) and asks for diagnostics
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and implementation fidelity, seeking evidence that claimed speedups and accuracy are robust, reproducible, and practically meaningful.

reviewer_b.name: Research Contextualizer
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor and NLP researcher with a decade of work on reading comprehension, efficient sequence models, and structured attention mechanisms, with a track record of papers assessing novelty and broader impact.
traits: values clear positioning relative to prior work on hard/soft attention, adaptive computation, hierarchical/skip RNNs, and non-differentiable control in sequence models
assesses conceptual novelty and theoretical plausibility (when and why learned jumps should preserve accuracy) and looks for limitations or failure modes across task types
considers broader impact and applicability (which downstream tasks benefit, whether method generalizes to long documents or multimodal inputs, and interpretability of jump decisions)
notices whether empirical claims advance the field (new capability, insight, or practical tool) and whether connections to related literature are thorough and fair
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's novelty, relation to prior art, and potential impact, highlighting where the method offers meaningful advances or where conceptual/empirical gaps remain.
